title: Will the Fishermen's Problem See the Light of Dawn?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The translation preserves the source's attributed statements, competing prisoner-release figures and chronology. Visually confirmed OCR repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: The Central government wrote letter after letter to Tamil Nadu about releasing Tamil Nadu fishermen languishing in Sri Lankan prisons, the continuing attacks upon them by the Sri Lankan Navy, other major fishing problems and a meeting between fishermen's representatives of the two countries. After pressure from every side, the AIADMK government has finally come forward to hold that meeting on the 20th of this month.

But where will it take place? Who will participate? When this important problem is discussed, should not the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister attend on behalf of our fishermen? Since she is at Kodanad, will the talks proceed without her? Is that the extent of her concern? One report says the representatives will meet in Delhi, another that they will meet in Chennai. No one has yet clarified which is true.

Meanwhile, *Dina Thanthi* reports that the meeting is taking place through Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's efforts. Even after reports that the Prime Minister had written repeatedly and that the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister and government caused the delay, the paper strangely pleads her case by crediting her efforts. Today's *Dina Thanthi* also carries a boxed report saying she wrote 33 letters and that, through her strenuous efforts, all Tamil Nadu fishermen in Sri Lankan prisons are expected to be released successively from today. It struggles mightily to place her at the summit of praise.

For Indian fishermen's welfare, I sent Parliamentary Party leader T.R. Baalu to Nagapattinam, had him end their hunger strike, and brought their representatives to Delhi to meet the Prime Minister and External Affairs Minister. It was there that the Prime Minister assured them that representatives of the two countries would meet around 20 January. *Dina Thanthi* conveniently conceals all this and sings “Amma's” praise. Poor things. What pressure must they be under?

Following sustained pressure, the Indian and Sri Lankan External Affairs Ministers recently discussed a solution by telephone. A delegation headed by Sri Lankan Fisheries Minister Rajitha Senaratne then held talks with External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid and met Union Agriculture Minister Sharad Pawar.

Afterward, Senaratne said: “It has been decided to release immediately the fishermen imprisoned in both countries. India will progressively release 160 Sri Lankan fishermen and four confiscated boats. Sri Lanka will likewise progressively release 236 Indian fishermen and 90 confiscated boats. Releases begin tomorrow. There is no truth in the allegation that the Sri Lankan Navy attacks Tamil Nadu fishermen.”

Yet only two days earlier in Sri Lanka, the same minister had said: “Tamil Nadu fishermen in Sri Lankan prisons will not be released before the bilateral talks. A solution must release detained fishermen on both sides simultaneously. Our economy suffers because Tamil Nadu fishermen enter Sri Lankan waters illegally and fish there.”

Nor is that all. While the minister was holding talks in Delhi, Indian fishermen were fishing southeast of Kodiyakkarai near Vedaranyam in Nagapattinam district. A Sri Lankan Navy patrol boat arrived and its personnel showered stones upon our fishermen, then cut and threw away all their nets. Even as efforts were underway for an amicable settlement, the Sri Lankan Navy launched an attack. Yet in Delhi the minister denied that Indian fishermen were attacked.

Tamil Nadu newspapers on the 14th reported that 163 of the 275 Tamil Nadu fishermen in Sri Lankan prisons were released on the 13th, and that 52 Sri Lankan fishermen in Tamil Nadu prisons were released the same day. Later reports said the 52 Sri Lankan fishermen had been released several days earlier; Sri Lanka had released 52 fishermen from Karaikal, Nagapattinam and Pudukkottai; and although a Sri Lankan court ordered the release of 162 Indians, Sri Lanka initially freed only 52 because Tamil Nadu had released only 52 Sri Lankans. Comparing these reports with the minister's present figures leaves the truth a puzzle. The Jayalalithaa government must clarify the actual position in the interests of Tamil Nadu fishermen.

Newspapers also report that a six-member committee has been formed to resolve major fishing issues, with three officials from each country, headed respectively by India's Agriculture Secretary and Sri Lanka's Director General of Fisheries, and that its first talks are to occur today, 16 January 2014. There is no explanation of the Tamil Nadu government's role. The AIADMK government must explain that too.

The Central and State governments must act with great vigilance and care in all these talks. No one can forget that the Sinhala government continually says one thing and does another, appearing as the embodiment of contradiction. During the 2009 war, it convinced the world and India that it had stopped the fighting, but continued it and destroyed the Liberation Tigers. We can neither forget nor forgive that.
After the war, whenever the Thirteenth Amendment was discussed, President Rajapaksa guaranteed that Tamils would receive powers beyond even that amendment. What followed was the opposite.

Rajapaksa promised elections and democratic government in the Northern Province. He held the election, but the Sinhala parties did not win as he dreamed. They suffered a crushing defeat, while the Tamil National Alliance won an absolute majority and formed the administration. Even then, it did not receive the minimum powers it expected.

Speaking at a conference in Jaffna last Friday, Northern Province Chief Minister Wigneswaran said: “The military obstructs the Northern administration. The province has become filled with camps. Many displaced families still cannot return home. The military has seized private and agricultural land. Though the land belongs to them, local farmers are forced to buy produce cultivated there by soldiers. Women and children suffer military intimidation. Infrastructure is inadequate. Differences arise with the Governor, a former Sri Lankan Army major general. Employees heed only the Governor.”

Jaffna MP and *Uthayan* proprietor Saravanapavan similarly told an interviewer in Chennai: “Even after the war, conditions remain unchanged. Military interference has not diminished. They promised rehabilitation for former fighters, but created neither proper employment nor livelihood. Two lakh people were removed from barbed-wire camps, but now suffer without proper homes or means of living.”

Union Agriculture Minister and our friend Sharad Pawar said on 15 January: “India and Sri Lanka have decided to form a departmental committee to examine detained fishermen's cases. I hope we will see some results next week. If not, the next options will be considered.” If further action follows as he says, every side will be pleased.

But the statements of Wigneswaran and Saravanapavan directly contradict Rajapaksa's earlier assurances. Even after two resolutions in the UN Human Rights Council, contrary acts continue in Sri Lanka. The Indian government must keep this in mind, remain highly vigilant in the coming talks, secure Tamil Nadu fishermen's welfare, and help them live peacefully without further Sri Lankan Navy cruelty.
Affectionately,
M.K.
17 January 2014
